The
Token
Age
Bobby Madamanchi · University of Michigan School of Information
Many of you have experienced
a brutal hiring market.
Entry-level and junior roles are harder to land than they were three years ago. This is real and it is not your imagination. But the explanation matters — because different causes have very different implications for your career strategy.
Is this a short-term phenomenon? Or a sign of things to come? There is genuine disagreement. Let's look at what we actually know.
What's driving this?
Cyclical — will resolve
01
Interest Rates
Higher cost of capital tightens headcount.
02
COVID Unwind
2021–22 overhiring surge correcting.
Structural — may not resolve
03
LLM Signal Noise
AI applications flood hiring pipelines, destroying signal. Employers retreat entirely.
04
LLM Displacing Junior Work
AI can now do baseline knowledge work. Junior roles feel it first.
Today we're focusing on 3 and 4.
You are going
to be OK.
You have real social capital — a Michigan masters degree, a technical skillset, and a network that most people navigating this disruption don't have access to. This moment will be stressful and the landscape is genuinely different. But you are among the best positioned people to handle it.

Understanding this landscape clearly — rather than through either panic or false reassurance — is itself an advantage. That's what we're here to do.

LLMs are flooding the signal.
Employers are going dark.
Pre-LLM: cover letter quality predicted work quality. Good writers got jobs.
Post-LLM: cover letter quality is useless as signal. Everyone's looks the same. Hiring rates fall.
High-ability workers lose the most — they can no longer distinguish themselves through writing.
Firms are barraged by AI applications and can't distinguish who is good — so they decide it's just not worth trying.

This is the Tinderization of the job market — infinite low-cost swipes destroy the signal that made the market function.

Matt Darling in The Argument: the job market worked because applications were a costly signal — it took real effort to apply well. LLMs destroyed the cost of that signal overnight.
The result isn't just more bad applications. It's that employers can no longer trust any application. Good candidates and bad candidates look identical from the outside. The market seizes up.

Read: The Tinder-ization of the Job Market
theargumentmag.com/p/the-tinder-ization-of-the-job-market

The solution is a
costlier signal.
When cheap signals become noise, the market shifts toward signals that are hard to fake. You need to demonstrate capability in ways AI cannot replicate on your behalf.
Portfolio work — real projects with real outputs that show judgment, not just execution
Network proximity — someone who knows your actual work vouching for you cuts through the noise entirely
Domain specificity — applying to roles where you have genuine depth is more legible than broad applications
We'll address this directly
The personal branding exercise at the end of this session is specifically about building the kind of costly signal that cuts through AI noise.
Factor 4 · Early Evidence
Sweden · AI Exposure by Age
Young (22–25) in high-AI roles: steep decline post-ChatGPT
Older workers and low-AI roles: stable or rising
First quasi-experimental evidence of LLM displacement
US · Computer Occupations by Age
Brynjolfsson et al. (2025): Early Career (22–25) drops sharply post-2022
All other age groups: flat or growing
Two countries, same pattern: youngest workers bear the cost
Why junior roles
are first in line.
The traditional value proposition of junior employees: they do the tedious grunt work, badly, but their effort makes up for it. Senior employees supervise, edit, and mentor. The eternal complaint: by the time juniors are trained up enough to be useful, they get promoted or leave.
Now it's easier to get replacement-level basics from an LLM — sometimes. At the same time, evolving expectations from the youngest employees make them more averse to scut work than ever.
This has the potential to get significantly worse as token costs approach zero.
Maybe AI is just
normal technology.
Jevons Paradox: when you make something more efficient, total consumption goes up, not down. Steam engines made coal more efficient — total coal consumption exploded. If AI makes data science cheaper, you may get vastly more data science done across industries that couldn't afford it. More surface area, not less.
But as token costs approach zero, the Mag 7's share of the economy may actually shrink relative to what they unlock. Capital flows to high asset, low obsolescence industries — waste management, manufacturing, physical infrastructure — things that don't get disrupted by cheaper tokens.

This is the historically grounded case. It's not obviously wrong. The question is whether this time the technology is different enough that Jevons doesn't fully offset displacement.

If AI broadly hollows out
knowledge work —
the strategy changes.
Kedrosky's conclusion: capital flows to high asset, low obsolescence industries. Waste management. Manufacturing. Physical infrastructure. Things that don't get disrupted by cheaper tokens because they require physical presence, capital equipment, and embodied knowledge.
Jevons / Normal Tech
More data science, not less
Cheap AI unlocks markets that couldn't afford software before. Build domain depth and find those markets.
Kedrosky / Bearish
Move toward physical assets
Knowledge work gets commoditized. Pair technical skills with industries where physical assets create moats.
Five analogies.
Five different lessons.
Every major technology disruption looked different in terms of what happened to labor. The question isn't whether AI will change work — it's which historical pattern we're actually in. Nobody knows yet.
For each one: look it up, discuss with your neighbor, add what you think the lesson is to the shared doc. Then I'll share my interpretation.

tinyurl.com/TokenAgeDiscussion

Analogy 1 of 5
The Green
Revolution
Post-WWII agricultural transformation. Fertilizer, mechanization, and new crop strategies dramatically increased yields.
What lessons can we draw?

tinyurl.com/TokenAgeDiscussion

Analogy 1 of 5 · My Take
Green Revolution:
Sectoral shift.
We went from ~40% of Americans in agriculture to under 2%. This was considered dystopic at the time.
In hindsight — was it all bad? Manufacturing and services expanded and absorbed the workforce. Living standards rose.
But tractors couldn't follow farm workers into factories. AI can follow knowledge workers almost anywhere. That's a potential difference.

My take: sectoral shifts can be survivable — if there's somewhere to go. The question is whether there's an absorbing sector this time.

Analogy 2 of 5
The Industrial
Revolution
What happened and how did it affect people? What lessons can we draw?

tinyurl.com/TokenAgeDiscussion

Analogy 2 of 5 · My Take
The Industrial
Revolution
Long-run: rising standard of living, new industries, aggregate prosperity. The textbook positive outcome.
Short-run: the Luddite movement showed the real damage to skilled craftsmen. They weren't anti-technology — they were defending their craft and their livelihood.
Real wages fell for decades even as aggregate output rose. The transition lasted a generation.

My take: you are living in the short run. Don't let long-run optimism obscure short-run damage.

Analogy 3 of 5
The Railroad
Boom
Mid-19th century. Massive capital investment in rail infrastructure. Connected national markets.
How does this apply to the moment we're in?

tinyurl.com/TokenAgeDiscussion

Railroads:
Value at the application layer.
Most capex went into rail — most investors lost money. Value accrued downstream: steel, refrigerated food, national retail, Sears catalog
The technology unlocked economic activity that literally couldn't exist before national markets connected
The hyperscalers are building the railroad right now. Nvidia is the steel company. The Sears catalog hasn't been built yet.

My take: don't try to build infrastructure. Build what the infrastructure makes possible. Agentic commerce, niche software, domain-specific tools — that's the application layer opportunity.

Analogy 4 of 5
The Electrification
of Industry
How did the arrival of electricity affect the economy and workers?

tinyurl.com/TokenAgeDiscussion

Electrification:
The time delay.
Factories swapped steam engines for electric motors — same layout, different power. Minimal gains.
Gains came when factories were redesigned from scratch around distributed power — new layouts, flexible assembly, smaller workshops
The firms using AI as a better search engine are at "swap the steam engine." The firms redesigned around AI don't exist yet.

My take: the biggest opportunities are 5–15 years out when organizations fully reorganize around AI. The depth you build now positions you to lead that reorganization. Don't optimize for 2025 — optimize for 2030.

Analogy 5 of 5
How did the rise of the
personal computer affect
white collar work?
The PC arrived in the mid-to-late 20th century.

tinyurl.com/TokenAgeDiscussion

Analogy 5 of 5 · My Take
The PC Transition:
Work moves up the stack.
Secretaries weren't just clerical — they co-produced. They managed relationships, anticipated needs, shaped how work flowed. That tacit contribution got absorbed and made invisible.
Cultural change plus cheap technology doubled output expectations per knowledge worker without doubling pay. Everyone got a computer and was expected to do everything.
AI as intellectual sparring partner — could restore some of that co-production at a higher level. Or it could just mean everyone is expected to do even more alone.

My take: the secretary question is the junior data scientist question. Absorption or disappearance? We don't know yet. That's why building depth now matters.

New Research · Job Transformation
Jobs are bundles of tasks.
Freund & Mann (2026): when AI automates a task, the remaining tasks gain in relative importance. Wage effects depend on your full skill profile — not just your job title.
High AI exposure → sharply negative wage effects on average
AI raises the value of social and manual skills while reducing returns to pure analytical skills
AI is mildly progressive — benefits low earners more than high earners

Winners are people whose remaining tasks become more valuable: new entrants with high-touch knowledge work, people-facing data roles, those who combine technical depth with social skills.

This isn't fate.
It's a political decision.
In the 1990s, deindustrialization hit every developed economy. Outsourcing and automation hollowed out manufacturing. Germany used apprenticeship systems and works councils to manage the transition. The US let the market rip — and we're still living with the consequences. We don't want to repeat that with knowledge work.
Every firm slashing junior roles is acting rationally. But the sector is eating its seed crop — destroying the pipeline that produces the next generation of senior expertise. What institutions can we develop to handle this collective problem?
Guilds arose in medieval Europe to protect craft knowledge and training across firm boundaries. Unions emerged during industrialization to give workers collective voice against displacement. These tools give us a guide for the future — the question is what form they take in the age of AI and bossless work.
Ok. Enough macro.
Let's talk about you. Here's my journey.
PhD
Cancer Biology
Vanderbilt. Systems thinking, messy biological data, living with uncertainty.
Post-doc
Computational Systems Biology
Purdue. Multicellular simulation, digital nucleus models. Depth compounds.
Post-doc
Future of Work & Learning
Purdue Polytechnic. First pivot — biology expertise meets workforce questions.
Now
Lecturer + Founder + Organizer
UMSI data science. PlanMate edtech startup. LEO-AFT union steward.
None of this was planned. Each step was possible because genuine depth at one stage opened the next. Now let's work through where you are with your neighbors.
What domain do you know that most data scientists don't?
